planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or divide established clumps in spring or fall in very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, lean soil; blue oat grass is a cool-season grass that suffers in hot, humid summers — in zones 7–9 it may go semi-dormant in summer and is best planted in a location with afternoon shade and excellent drainage to discourage crown rot.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, lean soil in full sun; blue oat grass is one of the most dramatically colored ornamental grasses available, producing a tight, arching mound of steely blue-gray, semi-evergreen foliage as intensely glaucous as any blue-leafed hosta or blue spruce. The narrow, stiff, steel-blue leaves emerge fresh in cool spring weather and catch low-angle light magnificently. In early summer, tall, arching flower spikes resembling oat grains emerge in a paler tan color that gradually dries and persists attractively into fall. It is native to the rocky, calcareous mountains of southern France and northwestern Spain and requires excellent drainage to survive — clay or moist soils lead to crown rot within a few seasons. Superb as a specimen or repeated accent in gravel gardens, xeriscape plantings, and as a blue foliage contrast to golden or red-leafed plants.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, lean soil in full sun; giant feather grass requires excellent drainage and dislikes waterlogged soils especially in winter. In zones 7–9 it grows as a semi-evergreen perennial and performs best in Mediterranean-style dry-summer gardens.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; giant feather grass is a spectacular large-scale ornamental grass from the Iberian Peninsula producing a semi-evergreen mound of narrow, arching, dark-green leaves from which arise in early summer enormously tall, delicate, transparent flower stalks — reaching 5–6 feet — bearing open, airy, glittering panicles of large golden oat-spikelets that catch and transmit sunlight with a shimmering, jewel-like quality unlike any other grass. The effect is breathtaking when backlit by morning or evening sun and the dried seed heads persist attractively through summer. Native to dry, limestone hillsides of Spain and Portugal, it is perfectly adapted to poor, well-drained, alkaline soils and thrives in drought conditions once established. It makes a spectacular specimen plant or repeated accent in the dry border, gravel garden, or Mediterranean-style planting where it provides transparent, see-through height without blocking the view.

planting.note: Divide established clumps or plant container-grown plants in spring or fall; blue sedge spreads slowly by rhizomes and can be used as a groundcover under trees where it gradually fills in. It tolerates both dry shade and moist shade as well as alkaline soils — one of the most adaptable of all sedges.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist soil from full shade to full sun; blue sedge is a low-growing, rhizomatous spreading sedge native to Europe and northern Africa with attractive steel-blue to blue-green, semi-evergreen foliage that spreads gently into a fine-textured, low groundcover. Unlike many ornamental sedges that require consistent moisture, Carex flacca tolerates dry shade under trees quite well and even succeeds on thin chalk soils — making it far more adaptable than most shade groundcovers. The 'Blue Zinger' cultivar is a compact, intensely blue selection excellent as a groundcover or front-of-border edging. It looks particularly effective planted in large drifts under light-canopy trees where the blue foliage creates a cool, restful effect. Mow or shear lightly in early spring to remove old foliage and encourage fresh new growth.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants after last frost; orange sedge is a New Zealand native that tolerates mild frosts but is damaged below about 10°F. It grows well in a range of soils from well-drained to moderately moist and tolerates light shade, developing its best color in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist soil in part shade to full sun; orange sedge is a New Zealand native producing fine, arching, evergreen foliage that emerges olive-green then develops warm orange-tan to bronze-orange tones that intensify in cooler temperatures and full sun — providing year-round color interest that complements orange, bronze, and copper-toned companion plants. The fine, thread-like foliage has a delicate, airy quality that contrasts well with bolder-leaved plants. It makes an excellent container plant, where its warm tones complement dark-leafed heucheras, black mondo grass, and orange-toned tulips and perennials. Unlike the more tender New Zealand sedges, C. testacea handles brief dips to single-digit temperatures when established. The orange-bronze coloration intensifies through fall and winter, making it most striking during the cooler months.

planting.note: Divide established clumps or plant container-grown plants in spring in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil; fiber optic grass thrives in boggy conditions or at the water's edge and can be grown as an aquatic in shallow water (up to 2 inches deep). Keep consistently moist — it does not tolerate drought.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; fiber optic grass is a charming miniature rush-like plant producing dense mounds of very fine, thread-like, bright-green foliage tipped with tiny white to pale brownish flower spikelets that catch the light like the tip of a fiber optic strand — hence the common name. The effect is distinctly whimsical and appealing. It is excellent as a container plant for water features, terrariums, and bog gardens, thriving in consistently wet conditions at pond or stream edges, in water gardens, and as a houseplant in very bright light. In zones 8–11 it grows as a reliable evergreen perennial in wet conditions. Its compact, fine-textured form contrasts beautifully with broad-leafed aquatic plants and it makes an unusual cascading element in container plantings or hanging baskets where moisture is maintained.
